title: Gel marbling in a gelatin bath
subtitle: Marbling on a gelatin size for crisper pattern detail
excerpt: Marbling in a gelatin bath rather than carrageenan produces a firmer size where pigments spread less readily but hold pattern edges more crisply. Gelatin is the older of the two marbling sizes and suits stone patterns and concentric ring work well.
sourceNotes: Josef Halfer, The Progress of the Marbling Art (1893, English translation by William F. Goss, 1894): public domain. Halfer notes gelatin as an earlier marbling size predating carrageenan's rise to dominance in the European marbling trade. The practical method here is synthesised from Halfer's observations alongside general marbling surface-tension principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: The thickened water in a marbling tray that holds pigment drops on the surface long enough to be combed into a pattern and transferred to paper. Carrageenan and gelatin are the two common sizes.
Pre-treating paper with an alum solution before marbling. The alum makes the surface receptive to acrylic pigment; without it, the pattern lifts but does not adhere to the paper.

A natural surfactant added drop by drop to marbling pigment. It lowers the surface tension of the pigment drop, causing it to spread outward on the size surface. The amount added controls the diameter of each colour drop.
The process of hydrating leaf or powdered gelatin in cold water before dissolving it. Bloomed gelatin dissolves more evenly and produces a cleaner size with fewer lumps.

content.text: Most contemporary marbling tutorials use carrageenan as the
size

. Gelatin is the older alternative, noted by Halfer in his documentation of the European marbling trade in the late 19th century. Gelatin produces a slightly firmer size than carrageenan. Pigments spread more slowly and their edges stay sharper. The trade-off is that the gelatin bath has a shorter working life (use it the same day it is prepared) and the temperature matters: the size must be cool but not set when marbling begins. Once you understand these two constraints, gelatin marbling is straightforward.

attrs.title: Marbling chemistry
attrs.body: Alum (potassium aluminium sulphate) used for mordanting paper is a mild skin and eye irritant. Wear gloves when mixing or applying it; rinse splashes promptly. Acrylic pigments are water-clean-up. Do not eat or drink at the marbling tray.

items.name: Leaf gelatin or powdered gelatin
items.qty: 1 tablespoon powdered or 4 leaves per 500 ml water
items.substitutions: Food-grade gelatine leaf (platinum grade) dissolves cleanly. Powdered gelatin works equally well.

items.name: Marbling acrylic, liquid
items.substitutions: Pre-thinned marbling inks; standard acrylic paints thinned with water to a single-cream consistency also work.

items.name: Ox-gall
items.substitutions: Synthetic ox-gall substitute (available from marbling suppliers). Dish soap is an emergency substitute but gives less controlled spread.

items.name: Marbling paper, alum-mordanted
items.qty: As many sheets as you intend to marble
items.substitutions: Pre-mordant your own sheets: dissolve 2 tablespoons of alum in 500 ml warm water, apply to the paper surface with a sponge, and allow to dry fully before marbling.

items.name: Marbling tray, at least A4 size
items.substitutions: A clean food-storage tub large enough for your paper.

content.text: The move
Prepare the gelatin size. Sprinkle powdered gelatin over cold water (or
bloom

leaf gelatin in cold water for five minutes). Then warm the water gently, stirring, until the gelatin dissolves completely. Do not boil. Pour into the marbling tray and allow the size to cool to room temperature. The size should be liquid, not set. If it has set, warm it gently to return it to a liquid state, then cool again. A firm set means the marbling tray temperature was too cold; move to a warmer room.

Test each pigment colour on the size surface before marbling. Add one small drop of pigment to the centre of the tray. It should spread into a circle of roughly 2-5 cm diameter. If it does not spread, add
ox-gall
to the pigment, one drop at a time, until the spread is right. On gelatin size, pigments spread slightly less than on carrageenan, so you may need marginally more ox-gall than you are used to.

Skim the surface of the size with a strip of newspaper to remove dust and the fine skin that forms on gelatin when exposed to air. This skim clears any contamination that would cause resist spots in the pattern.

Drop the colours onto the surface in the pattern you want. For a stone pattern, drop colours randomly across the tray surface. For concentric ring work (similar to suminagashi), drop one colour in the centre, then drop a second colour in the middle of the first circle, and continue alternating. On gelatin size the rings are tighter and more defined than on carrageenan.
Take the
mordanted

paper and lower it onto the surface in one smooth movement, centre first. Hold the paper at the two short ends, bow it slightly in the middle, and lower the centre first, then release the ends so the paper lies flat on the size. Avoid air bubbles.
After five to ten seconds, lift the paper from the far short edge, peeling it up and toward you in a steady movement. The pattern transfers to the paper surface as it lifts off the size.

Rinse the marbled sheet gently under slow-running cold water to remove any residual size from the surface. Hold the sheet at one short end and run the water along the sheet from the other end toward you, tilting the sheet so the water flows across and off rather than pooling. Lay face-up on a flat surface to dry.
Working life of the gelatin bath

Gelatin size works well for several hours at room temperature, then begins to thin as the gelatin degrades. On warm days (above 22°C), the working life is shorter. Mix the size on the day you intend to use it and plan to complete your marbling session within three to four hours. The tray can be refrigerated and re-used the following day, but re-warming it for use the next day risks over-thinning the size. Carrageenan, by contrast, keeps in the refrigerator for up to a week. If you need a multi-session material, carrageenan is more practical.

items.problem: Pigment sinks below the surface instead of spreading
items.cause: Pigment too heavy or insufficiently thinned; or the gelatin size is too thin
items.fix: Thin the pigment further with water. Ensure the pigment contains enough ox-gall to float on the surface tension. If the size itself is too thin (the gelatin degraded), mix a fresh batch.

items.problem: Pattern tears or smears when lifting the paper
items.cause: Paper placed too slowly, trapping air; or paper left on the size too long before lifting
items.fix: Lower the paper in one confident movement and lift after no more than ten seconds. Leaving the paper too long allows the gelatin to partially set around the sheet.

items.problem: Blotchy areas where pigment has not adhered
items.cause: Paper not properly mordanted; or the alum solution on the paper surface has crystallised
items.fix: Check that the mordanted sheets are fully dry before marbling. If alum crystals are visible as a white bloom on the surface, the alum concentration was too high. Dilute and re-mordant.

content.text: Where it leads

Gelatin size suits stone and ring patterns well. For combed patterns (nonpareil, French combed, bouquet), carrageenan is more reliable because the longer working life allows more time between skim and comb. The two marbling traditions share the same papers, the same mordanting process, and the same pigments: once comfortable with one size, switching to the other is straightforward.
